title: Today's AI Brief: Reclaiming Social Engineering for Good
summary: Exploring the reclaiming of social engineering for good, Groq's $650 million raise, and more
imageAlt: Image from source article for Reclaiming Social Engineering for Good

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. We're looking at some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, the term 'social engineering' often brings to mind phishing scams and other malicious activities. But what if I told you that researchers are working on reclaiming social engineering for good? It's a fascinating area that caught my attention.

According to a recent article in IEEE Spectrum, researchers are exploring ways to use social engineering for beneficial purposes. This involves understanding how people interact with each other and with technology, and using that knowledge to design more effective and trustworthy AI systems. The idea is to turn the tables on scammers and create AI that helps people, not just companies. For instance, imagine AI-powered tools that can detect and prevent online harassment or help people make more informed decisions.

In other news, AI chip startup Groq is raising $650 million in funding. This significant investment is a vote of confidence in the company's vision for more efficient and powerful AI processing. Groq's second act is certainly one to watch, especially since their chips could potentially accelerate the development of more sophisticated AI models. I'm curious to see how their technology will shape the future of AI.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: Scoop: Groq raising $650 million for its second act - Axios
